perturbation.original_text: Either party may terminate this MNDA without cause upon five (5) days written notice given to the other, provided that confidentiality obligations under Section A of this Attachment A shall survive termination hereof.
perturbation.changed_text: No rights or obligations other than expressly recited herein are to be implied here from. Nothing except that expressly stated herein shall affect either party's present or prospective rights under any country's patent laws, or be construed as granting any license under any present or future patent or application therefor, or preclude marketing any product unless such marketing constitutes unauthorized disclosure of INFORMATION.
perturbation.explanation: Moving the termination clause to the section dealing with implied rights obscures the explicit termination right, which is standard in contracts.
perturbation.law_explanation: M.G.L. c. 93A, § 2 prohibits unfair or deceptive acts or practices in trade or commerce. Obscuring a standard termination clause can be considered a deceptive practice, as it misleads a party about their ability to end the agreement. While 93A doesn't explicitly require termination clauses, hiding them creates an unfair imbalance of power, potentially violating the spirit of the law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: General Law - Part I, Title XV, Chapter 93A, Section 2 Skip to Content General Laws Part I ADMINISTRATION OF THE GOVERNMENT Title XV REGULATION OF TRADE Chapter 93A REGULATION OF BUSINESS PRACTICES FOR CONSUMERS PROTECTION Section 2 Unfair practices; legislative intent; rules and regulations Section 2 Search Search the Legislature Search General Laws Go Directly to a General Law Chapter Section Go to General Law Skip to Content Print Page Prev ious Next Section 2: Unfair practices; legislative intent; rules and regulations Section 2. (a) Unfair methods of competition and unfair or deceptive acts or practices in the conduct of any trade or commerce are hereby declared unlawful. (b) It is the intent of the legislature that in construing paragraph (a) of this section in actions brought under sections four, nine and eleven, the courts will be guided by the interpretations given by the Federal Trade Commission and the Federal Courts to section 5(a)(1) of the Federal Trade Commission Act (15 U.S.C. 45(a)(1)), as from time to time amended. (c) The attorney general may make rules and regulations interpreting the provisions of subsection 2(a) of this chapter. perturbation.justification: The original text contains an explicit termination clause, while the changed text moves this clause to a section that deals with implied rights, obscuring the party's right to terminate the contract. M.G.L. c. 93A, § 2 prohibits unfair or deceptive acts or practices in trade or commerce, according to the Law Explanation. The Perturbation Explanation notes that obscuring a standard termination clause can be considered a deceptive practice. The scraped snippets define unfair or deceptive acts or practices in the conduct of any trade or commerce as unlawful according to Massachusetts General Laws Chapter 93a, Section 2 (2022) and General Law - Part I, Title XV, Chapter 93A, Section 2. Therefore, obscuring the termination clause is a structural flaw that creates a deceptive practice and contradicts the law.

perturbation.original_text: Consistent with other provisions herein, each party assures that it will not knowingly, without obtaining prior authorization from the U.S. Dept. of Commerce Office of Export Administration, transmit directly or indirectly the technical data received pursuant hereto or the immediate product (including processes and services) produced directly by use of such technical data to Afghanistan, People's Republic of China, or any other Country Group Q, S, W, T, or Z country specified in Supplement No. I to Part 370 of U.S. Dept. of Commerce Export Administration Regulations.
perturbation.changed_text: This MNDA shall not be modified or changed in any manner except in a writing signed by both parties.
perturbation.explanation: The clause regarding export restrictions has been placed in a section that covers modification of the agreement, therefore, reducing its visibility and potential enforcement.
perturbation.law_explanation: 15 C.F.R. § 732.1 outlines steps for determining export control requirements. By burying the export restriction clause, the contract obscures important compliance information, potentially leading to unintentional violations of export regulations.

perturbation.justification: The original text contains a clause regarding export restrictions, which is a legal requirement. The changed text moves this clause to a section covering modifications of the agreement. The Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation suggest this placement obscures compliance, potentially leading to unintentional violations of export regulations outlined in 15 C.F.R. § 732.1. Scraped Law Snippet 2 also highlights the importance of the Export Administration Regulations (EAR) under 15 CFR 730-774. The modification creates a structural flaw by burying a legally required disclosure in an irrelevant section, making it difficult to locate and interpret, thus contradicting the need for clear compliance.

perturbation.original_text: This document and appendices contain the entire agreement between the Parties and supersede any previous oral or written understandings, commitments or agreements pertaining to the subject matter hereof.
perturbation.changed_text: This MNDA shall be construed, interpreted and applied in accordance with the laws of the Commonwealth of Massachusetts.
perturbation.explanation: The entire agreement clause has been swapped with choice of law. This obscures the clause which limits the contract's scope to what is written, limiting legal recourse.
perturbation.law_explanation: UCC § 2-202 governs the parol evidence rule, which generally states that if a contract is intended to be the complete and exclusive statement of the terms of the agreement, evidence of any prior agreement or contemporaneous oral agreement may not be admitted to vary the contract. Moving the 'entire agreement' clause to a less visible location reduces its prominence, potentially allowing extrinsic evidence to be improperly admitted in court, thus contradicting the function of the clause.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-202. Final Written Expression: Parol or Extrinsic Evidence. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-202. Final Written Expression: Parol or Extrinsic Evidence. Terms with respect to which the confirmatory memoranda of the parties agree or which are otherwise set forth in a writing intended by the parties as a final expression of their agreement with respect to such terms as are included therein may not be contradicted by evidence of any prior agreement or of a contemporaneous oral agreement but may be explained or supplemented (a) by course of dealing or usage of trade (Section 1-205 ) or by course of performance (Section 2-208 ); and (b) by evidence of consistent additional terms unless the court finds the writing to have been intended also as a complete and exclusive statement of the terms of the agreement . perturbation.justification: The original contract contained an entire agreement clause, limiting the contract's scope to what is written, which limits legal recourse. The changed text replaces this clause with a choice of law clause. According to the Law Explanation, this obscures the 'entire agreement' clause, potentially allowing extrinsic evidence to be improperly admitted in court. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing UCC § 2-202, states that terms intended as a final expression of agreement may not be contradicted by prior or contemporaneous agreements. By removing the entire agreement clause, the changed text weakens the protection against extrinsic evidence, directly contradicting the purpose of the clause and the UCC's parol evidence rule.
